@graph.name: BMW X4 M40d Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X4 M40d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B57 engine typically costs £6,000–£10,000 supply only depending on the year, with the 340 bhp facelift version sitting at £7,000–£12,000. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X4 M40d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B57 is rated ★★★☆☆. It's a powerful and refined engine, but it has known weak points: HPFP failure (especially on 2018–2020 cars), timing chain tensioner wear (addressed by BMW Service Bulletin SWE-19-07), turbocharger wear and actuator faults, and EGR carbon buildup. Diligent maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X4 M40d?

acceptedAnswer.text: HPFP failure (early cars), timing chain tensioner wear, turbocharger wear and actuator faults, and EGR carbon buildup are the most common. The HPFP is the most critical — if it fails catastrophically, it can destroy the entire fuel system and engine.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X4 M40d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept G02 X4 M40d worth £20,000–£35,000, an £8,000 recon is a manageable 23–40% of the car's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. Given the X4 M40d's strong retained values, replacement is usually viable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X4 M40d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The X4 M40d uses the B57 3.0-litre twin-turbo diesel engine. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the B57's timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement with reinforced units (£1,500–£3,000) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap (£6,000–£12,000 engine replacement). BMW Service Bulletin SWE-19-07 addresses this issue — ensure any used car has had this work completed.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW X4 M40d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replace with upgraded pump and revised camshaft lobes; catch it before debris destroys the engine
Replace with reinforced units per BMW Service Bulletin SWE-19-07
Specialist refurbishment or replacement; catch it before engine damage
Regular cleaning; use quality fuel and motorway runs to prevent
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X4 M40d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with documented oil-change intervals
* HPFP replaced with upgraded unit on early cars (2018–2020)
* Timing chain tensioner replaced with reinforced unit per Service Bulletin SWE-19-07
* 2024+ facelift for the 340 bhp output and mild-hybrid system
* Evidence of regular motorway use (to prevent EGR carbon buildup)
avoidIf: * Long crank times or hesitation — potential HPFP failure risk
* Cold-start rattle with no chain history — timing chain tensioner warning
* Loss of power or turbo noise without diagnosis
* Poor service history — the B57 is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X4 M40d is a remarkable performance diesel — 326–340 bhp from a six-cylinder twin-turbo. But the B57 has known weak points: HPFP failure (especially early cars), timing chain tensioner wear, and turbo issues. Buy on documented history and proof of preventative maintenance. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with upgraded HPFP and timing chain components is essential.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X4 M40d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle on startup (timing chain tensioner wear)
* Service History — Including which engine era (B57 is the only M40d engine — verify this before purchase)
* HPFP Health — Long crank times? Hesitation? HPFP failure is a critical risk on early cars
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration through the rev range; any lag, whistling, or uneven power delivery?
* EGR System — Any loss of power or rough idle? EGR carbon buildup is common
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 5,000 miles? If so, investigate
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures; often point to EGR or DPF problems
